Chronicle of Shadows

Zenith Rhal was not supposed to be exceptional — he was supposed to become exceptional. That distinction matters in Tu'narath, where arcanists-in-training are measured against one another with the cold precision of blades. He was talented but not the most talented and, narrow as that gap was, it became the crack through which his life shattered. The psionic heritage of his people was there, latent as banked fire, and the masters always warned that it was too close to what the illithids were, too close to what the gith had clawed free from. Zenith listened intently and then, in the quiet of a practice duel he was losing, he reached for it anyway.

The boost was small in the beginning, and he told himself it was a single use, a controlled experiment. The Intrusion, when it first came, was minor; a brief fracture at the edge of perception, reality whispering at its seams. He noted it with scholarly detachment and did not stop. His spells grew stranger over the months that followed: harder to resist, their geometries subtly wrong, as though they arrived from an angle that did not exist in normal space. His masters noticed the results without being able to name the method. He was careful but he was also, in the precise clinical sense, addicted — not to power exactly, but to the sensation of the veil thinning, the feeling of knowing that something vast existed just beyond the edge of what could be seen. And, perhaps, also to power.

He was demonstrating a scrying when it happened. He had pushed farther than he meant to, the veil between worlds thinning unnoticed, and his vision went past the planes he knew and touched something that should not exist. The Lich-Queen Vlaakith CLVII was not scrying him, but she too was reaching into that strange place only beings of the Far Realm and those touched by them could see. In the half-second before he severed the contact, each of them saw the other clearly: her panic at being witnessed in an act anathema to his kind, his at what witnessing her would cost him. The githyanki are not without internal dissent, and the long years under the lich-queen's rule grated on more than a few powerful factions in Tu'narath. But what he had witnessed was something that would end her rule and shake their society with the outrage it would inflame, and he could not stay in Tu'narath without dying for it.

He ran. His portal, seized by the eldritch forces he had been quietly feeding for months, did not take him where he intended but did take him to one of the few places Vlaakith could not easily touch.

He arrived in Barovia in the grey hour before a dawn that never quite came. The disorientation was brief, for a githyanki arcanist's first instinct is classification, and Barovia classified itself quickly: a closed domain of near-absolute control built around one sovereign will. He had grown up in Tu'narath and recognised the structure. What he had not expected was how fast he would be found.

Madam Eva found him within two days, already knowing his name, and told him one useful truth: the lord of this place would come eventually, and his only asset was to be more interesting alive than dead. Muriel Vinshaw, a Keeper of the Feather, had reached the site of his arrival before Strahd's agents and gave him something more practical: patrol routes, safe houses, the names of roads that he struggled to learn. It was not enough, for after a mere fifty-three days of roaming Barovia and its settlements, he heard Eva's voice in his head urging him to run — and run he did, straight into Rahadin.

What He Knows

The vampire lord loves someone dearly and has lost them. A strange aura cloaks him, familiar - almost kin to the dark powers Zenith himself beseeches. Whispers in the dark mention to him a temple of amber somehwere in the Mists, a place of communion where he might call down eldritch might from beyond the stars and escape this plane. He knows too of silver knights shining in the dark. Perhaps a cautionary tale for him.

What He Doesn't Know

To be decided by the DM.
